Dear julesou@bluemail.ch,



I regret to inform you that there has been a security breach involving the devices you use for internet browsing.



Several months ago, unauthorized access was gained to these devices, allowing me to monitor your internet activity. Recently, I managed to hack your email accounts, including your password: MArcel2342.



Furthermore, a Trojan virus has been installed on all devices you use to access email. This was made possible due to your clicking on links from emails in your mailbox, which facilitated my penetration into your systems. Through this malicious software, I gained access to various features of your devices, such as the microphone, video camera, and keyboard. Additionally, I extracted and stored your personal information, data, photos, and web browsing history on my servers. Moreover, I gained access to your messengers, social networks, email, chat history, and contact list.



To remain undetected, my virus continuously updates its signatures, making it invisible to antivirus software.



During my investigation, I discovered that you frequently visit adult websites and view explicit content. I managed to record your intimate moments and create a montage showcasing them. If you doubt the authenticity of my claims, I can easily share these videos with your friends, colleagues, and relatives, or even make them publicly accessible.



I am convinced that it is in your best interest to prevent the disclosure of this information, given the potential consequences. Therefore, I propose the following solution: transfer $500 USD to my Bitcoin wallet (details provided below), based on the exchange rate at the time of the transaction. After the transfer is completed, all compromising information will be immediately deleted. After this, I will deactivate and remove the malicious software from your devices.



You can be assured that I will uphold my end of the agreement.



Bitcoin wallet: bc1q770895668ysry2l0ezqgjg2cax5e62u6k9vmg0



You have 48 hours. As soon as you open this email, I will receive a notification, and from that moment on, the countdown begins.



If you've never dealt with cryptocurrencies before, it's quite simple. Just type "cryptocurrency exchange" into a search engine, and you're good to go.



Please refrain from the following actions:



Replying to this email, as it was created in your mailbox and contains the sender's address.

Contacting the police or other security services. Discussing this situation with friends may lead to immediate public disclosure of the videos.

Attempting to identify me. All cryptocurrency transactions are anonymous.

Reinstalling the operating system or disposing of devices, as the videos are already stored on remote servers.

You need not worry about the following:



Receiving your funds transfer. My malicious program continuously monitors your actions.

Disseminating your videos after the funds transfer is completed. I have no intention of complicating your life further.

Finally, I strongly advise you to avoid similar situations in the future. Regularly change all your passwords to enhance online security.

Fake support calls from the spoofed numbers:
0449400635

call from +41 78 917 99 00
said they were from Microsoft and that my PC downloads keyloggers. when asked for more details they ended the call. however, they knew my name.

My elderly mother received a phone call from someone claiming to be from Swisscom technical support. They told her that her internet connection had been used for illegal activities and that she needed to pay 500 CHF in Neosurf vouchers to avoid prosecution. She bought the vouchers and gave them the codes before my brother intervened. The caller had a Swiss-German accent and knew her first name.
Caller said he was from Microsoft, about a problem with my computer. I asked why he thought I was that stupid to believe that crap and he said "because you are...", confirming it was a scam.

I received a phone call at 19.31 on Thursday 16th August from +41 44 824 7111.  The caller claimed to be calling from Swisscom and that they have complaints from the banks about spam emails coming from my ip address.  They wanted to arrange an appointment tomorrow to help diagnose the issue on my computer.  I thanked them and said I would run my own scans and contact swisscom myself.

A security check from "Microsoft" wanted me to give him the access to my computer to get rid of some malware. The person had a strong Indian/Asian accent, was very forceful and even downright rude trying to press me to follow his instructions. I was supposed to immediately install a remote access in his favour, at which point I sent him to blazes (the scam was at that moment too obvious). Anyone else would have complied as the person was very insistent. Quite a cunning scanner. As mentioned before, Microsoft (or another tech company) would NEVER CALL DIRECTLY ITS CUSTOMER.

Hi,
I guy with an Indian accent called me and he claimed to call from Microsoft. 
He was explaining that they have noticed based on "computer ID" or something that my Windows laptop had been hacked and that the hackers had accessed it several times over the past few days. 
I listened for a while, but then I told him that I am a professional and asked him to get another not a scammer job and that scamming people is not nice thing to do. I also told that I will report his call to the authorities (happening now). 

The call was coming from the number +41 79 8744445, but obviously, that is not the correct number. 

It would be interesting to know how much these calls come here in Switzerland and how much the operators and officials can do to stop these.

Call from a French speaking person telling me that my computer was sending her messages.Followed by other calls later on from the Police and from hidden phone number.My phone number : 0799384726 The malicious caller's number:+41 78 729 45 56

The caller identified himself as a Microsoft Support representative. He spoke in English and it was obvious that he was Indian because of his accent. Call occurred on August 26, 2024 09:02 local time.

I received a call today from +44.1268.709.559 (my phone identified the call as originating from "Basildon, England, United Kingdom.") The caller explained he was from "Windows Security Division" and that there had been a lot of bad traffic originating from my computer (which is an iMac by the way). I told him I was going to report the number to the Swiss government, which I am doing now. As soon as I hung up I got another call, this time from Italy, from +39.346.249.8227. I assumed this was the same character, just changing his dialling info, so I did not answer the phone. I have since blocked both numbers on my device. Thank you -- John Smith

I have a window that appears (Microsoft Windows) with a spoken message on loop that says my computer has been blocked because an identity theft attempt etc. has been made. I should under no circumstances turn off or restart the computer, and call urgently the displayed number 022 518 63 50.

Impossible to use the mouse. I had to turn off my computer by pressing and holding the key.

This message is very credible. I made a video of this message just in case but cannot attach it here except a photo.

Hello,

My mother (74) was checking the weather on meteoswiss.ch when her browser suddenly went fullscreen with a flashing red screen claiming "WINDOWS DEFENDER ALERT — Your PC has been infected with 3 viruses. DO NOT close this window. Call Microsoft Support immediately: +41 22 575 11 88." There was a very loud beeping sound she couldn't stop. She called me in tears thinking she had broken her computer. I came over and unplugged the laptop directly. After rebooting everything was fine. No virus scan found anything afterwards.

Just wanted to flag this since elderly people are particularly vulnerable to this scary alarm.

Regards
M. Sandberg

I was consulting the official website of the World Curling Championship currently taking place in Geneva, when the screen suddenly froze, several pages appeared one of which "informed me that my computer had been blocked as a precaution because there was a risk of hacking...". This message offered me to contact a free Microsoft service whose phone number was 022 539 44 05. I obviously did not dial this number and turned off my computer. I called Swisscom to find out the holder of this line, without result, it does not correspond to anything.

Do you have the possibility to call this number to find out more???

Thanking you for informing me of the follow-up you will give to this email I send you Madam, Sir, my best regards

I received an alarm on my laptop to say a security threat had been detected and to call technical support immediately. They said I had no anti virus installed and I needed to immediately order a CCleaner Pro. He gave me his name and ID number. He explained I had a network worm and he needed access remotely. I gave him this and put my credit card details into the site to buy the cleaner, after pressing the padlock symbol to show that I was the only one to be able to view this info. They had access to my laptop for hours whilst I was busy with my students and I was on the phone to them also for hours. I have a few photos and a video to share if necessary.

I was browsing the internet when a pop-up appeared saying my computer was infected and I had to call Microsoft immediately at 0800 123 456. I called and a man with a foreign accent asked me to install a remote access tool called AnyDesk. He then showed me my "infected" system files and asked for 250 CHF to clean the computer. I paid by credit card before realizing it was a scam.

My father visited the website www.motar-traffic.hi, when suddenly the window in the attachment opened. The window was automatically set to fullscreen. The window could be closed with Task Manager. It was indicated that one should call the number +41 22 593 22 18. What we can no longer reconstruct is whether he clicked on any advertisement or not.

Hello,

I am reporting a strange thing that happened to my MacBook. I was browsing recipes on a cooking blog when Safari suddenly opened a window saying "APPLE SUPPORT — YOUR MAC IS COMPROMISED — Your iCloud has been hacked. Call Apple Care immediately at +41 44 580 22 91 to prevent data loss."

I didn't know Apple worked this way through a webpage. I forced Safari to quit with cmd+option+esc. When I restarted Safari it asked to restore tabs, which I refused.

I'm a designer, I work on Mac, and I had never seen this kind of thing on Mac before. I had only heard about it on Windows. Wanted to report so others know it happens on Mac too.

Best regards
L. Petrović

Hello,

I need to report what happened to me this afternoon. I was reading articles on a gardening blog when suddenly my screen filled with a Microsoft Defender warning. A robotic voice kept repeating that my bank account was at risk and that I had to call +41 22 575 09 88 within five minutes or my account would be frozen by Microsoft.

I am 71 years old and I almost called. My grandson happened to call me about something else and I told him what was happening. He told me to just unplug the computer, which I did. After turning it back on everything was normal.

I want to thank you and also warn others. These people target older folks. The voice was very convincing and the sound of the alarm made me feel I was in real danger.

Regards,
W. Eriksson

Dear representatives,
My name is Michel Tocri.

On April 4, 2019, I made an account on a ‘crypto trade’ site: krakentokens.com/mobile/#/, following a link, sent on Telegram by Ander +447360253780, someone I don’t know in real life, who I got in touch with on Bumble.

On April 6th in the morning I realized the site was spam because the money I had tried to return to my Binance account on 15 April in the evening, as Ander taught me, hadn’t yet arrived in my account.

To register on this site, I used one of my e-mail addresses MichelTocri@gmail.com and for identification, I sent my Romanian passport photo and my Swiss driving license photo. I could not delete the account after I realized it was spam and at the moment I cannot access that account anymore with the login data I have.

I cancelled both my passport and my driving license on 9 June 2019 but I was wondering if you could tell me if there is anything else I should do to prevent my identity from being used for further possible crime, such as money laundering, especially as if they google my name they can easily find out exactly where I work and they might also have my phone number from Telegram. For example, is there something similar to CIFAS (UK) in Switzerland, where I could apply for protective registration?
I would also like to declare that I have only one bank account (Postfinance) and one credit Card (Cembra), as well as a Revolut account, where I send money using my Cembra card. I have no other accounts and no credits in Switzerland and abroad, I haven’t opened any since 4 March and I am not going to open any in the future.
I deleted the chat with Ander on both sides after taking some photos of the conversation and blocked him. On Tuesday he tried to contact me using a Chinese name (@guozi1998). Another girl I don’t know, called Evelyn (@Evelyn811), tried to contact me on Wednesday, because of the same reason I suppose, when the man saw that he couldn’t get in contact with me anymore. From the photo, she also looks Chinese and I suppose they both might be part of a larger network.
I would appreciate receiving a confirmation of receipt of this report at michitoc@gmail.com and saliva.saliva@bluewin.ch. I am willing to give further details and share photos related to the incident if this could be helpful in other similar situations as well. However, I would like my identity to be protected from public displays such as the news, for example.

Thank you very much for your assistance.
Kind regards,
Michel Tocri

Dear Madam / Sir,
I have just tried to report this through the online form but an error occurred.

I am working in the Modal Assurance team (Risk Management / Security) of flammèche Cosmetics, based in Basel.
During last weekend (31st march / 1st April), 1 database of our Group hosted by Microsoft Azure and containing personal data of customers has been attacked.
The adversary then accessed our mailing system (Sendgrid) and sent an email to some approximately 2,700 of our colleagues, in several waves yesterday and today. The emails were to inform of the attack, and to threaten to sell the stolen data if the company did not pay 45 BTC in ransom. You can find the emails in attachment.
We have established a crisis management team comprising of our internal IT teams in Switzerland and Czech Republic, our IT partners IBM and Microsoft, our Modal Assurance team in Switzerland, our Legal team, our CFO and CEO.

The first elements that we have collected indicate that the attacker compromised one of our web servers via trojans and got access to all data stored there, including credentials such as connection strings to storage accounts where we store documents containing personal data of flammèche Cosmetics customers. We are also able to confirm that the data was indeed downloaded by the adversary and then deleted from the database (approximately 4 TB).
Initial elements seem to indicate that the adversary is located in Russia and has been accessing a server and database primarily dedicated to the operation of our Russia and other ex-Soviet commercial markets.
We have taken immediate measures to limit any further damage. However at this stage we are not able to confirm whether the adversary is still in our infrastructure or not. We are currently informing relevant data protection authorities.
Please note that the data stolen does not concern – as far as we can tell – any Swiss data subjects, as our Group does not run any commercial operations in Switzerland.

Thank you and best regards,
Daniel Ratliff

All the files on our QNAP NAS unit (approx 2 TB) have been encrypted as of 06 feb 2012 (.7z suffix added to every file).  There is a text file in each folder demanding transfer of bitcoin to decrypt.  (We will not do this.) 

There are four user accounts; two-factor authentication is set up for the two main users but we have realised it was not set up for the two additional family members.

Is there anything we can do to retrieve the data?

I am selling used baby clothes at low prices on tutti.ch . Someone contacted me interested in an item. Tge agreed to pay via TWINT and gave me an address. I sent the item via Swiss post. Instead of paying me via TWINT, à fake company posing as tutti emailed me a link to a service for a TWINT transfer. It requested that i submit my ébanking details, my TWINT company, my credit card info.

Someone contacted me on Tutti showing interest in my item. She told me to write to her by WhatsApp (077 289 76 18).

After some chatting, she claimed to have gone to the post to pay for the postage and the money that would be paid to me (67chf) for the item. She sent me the (forged) receipt from the Post. There is a QR code and if I enter the website, it’s a fake Post website, but looking quite real. There is a button for me to click to claim my money. It led to a fake ZKB page and asked me to put in my login credentials. That’s when I was sure this was a scam.

I sold an item on Ricardo.ch. I was then contacted by email and SMS to provide further details to verify my account as it was my first sale. Since, I do not use Twint, they asked for copies of my Swiss ID card and my photos. This happened yesterday evening. What should I do now?

Attempted phishing fraud via the Tutti.ch platform. A supposed buyer ("Renate Gohl") contacted me via SMS to buy an item (Logitech keyboard). I was sent a professionally forged Swiss Post receipt (timestamped Good Friday, 03.04.2026, 15:08, even though post offices are closed). Additionally, I received a phishing link to the domain: swisspost.zahlung-10367020info.cfd/get/270797485 The goal was clearly to steal my TWINT or bank credentials via a fake website. I entered no data and blocked the user. user phone number +41772929222

I received a what's app message from a Swiss Number +41 76 758 02 16. This happened minutes after I listed an item on tutti.ch for sale. She asked me about my tutti.ch item for sale. Then asked me to confirm payment via this fraudulent link. This leads directly to people inserting their Bank account and Twint details. They prey on people new to Tutti.ch who do not know how the payment process works. They claim to come pick up the item in person and pressure you to confirm/click as soon as possible so that the item you sell is "reserved" and the sale confirmed.

I posted an ad on Anibis last week for a road bike. A few hours later I got a message from someone called "Lukas" who said he would buy it without negotiating the price. He said he wanted to pay via a delivery service and sent me a link that looked like Anibis but the URL was anibis-secure-payment.com or something similar. The page asked for my card number and CVV to "receive the payment". I almost entered everything but my wife stopped me. He also kept pushing me to move the conversation to WhatsApp and asked for a small 5 CHF postage confirmation first. Can you confirm this is a scam?

I received a Whatsapp message from a Ricardo user through this number (+41 79 807 59 03) saying that she had purchased an item from me, but the payment won’t go through because my account isn’t verified yet. Then I got an email from kundendienst@ricardomail.ch  asking me to verify my account and that a customer support will be in touch with me to go through the verification process. Then I received a WhatsApp message from someone who pretended to be from Ricardo team (+41 32 539 11 57). She asked me to go through a verification process by responding to the Ricardo emails, and I eventually went to a website that required the verification. As part of the verification process I was asked to fund my account for 1,000 CHF. I declined to do this because it’s impossible to manage that transfer. Eventually I was asked to go through another verification process and ended up sending a picture of my Swiss permit card. Both numbers stopped responding and I also eventually got another email from Ricardo that this might be a potential fraud. I have since then asked Ricardo to look into it but I haven’t gotten a response yet. I’m worried that my identity is being used for fraudulent activities.

She claims she can pay at the post for the object in question together with the shipping and I will then get a ticket. With this ticket I can go to the post and ship the object while cashing in the money.



My thoughts: This service is not available for private customers at the post, only a business can do it. I guess the scam is that she would then pay the amount to herself and I won‘t get anything. I tried to play along a bit but couldn’t find out more unfortunately.



I don’t know what you can do, I’m sure you have bigger fishes to catch. But she has a Swiss phone number you can maybe check.

They tell me that someone has requested an online credit in my name and I needed to tell them the Name of my bank so they can escalate yeh incident a reported to the police. I’ve never tell them any information required and proceed to end the call
